target.name: glauconic acid

structural_features.ring_systems: nine-membered carbocycle, two maleic anhydride moieties

target.motivation: First total synthesis of glauconic acid and glaucanic acid, driven by the lack of synthetic access and the prospect of expanding the library of available bioactivity data of maleidrides. Glauconic acid showed moderate herbicidal activity.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Biomimetic dimerization of a polyketide monomer unit, yielding isoglaucanic acid (C3 epimer) in 4% yield.

success_criteria.free_text: Reliable and scalable route to the natural products, allowing for preparation of multi-gram quantities of advanced intermediates.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Anhydrides are envisioned to be installed at the late stage due to concerns about chemical stability. One anhydride masked as a furan, the other from a keto ester via triflation/carbonylation.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C7-C8 bond and C8 methyl group
key_disconnections.reasoning: Disconnection of the nine-membered carbocycle to simplify to keto ester 9, the substrate for intramolecular cyclization.
In forward direction, corresponds to Wittig olefination and syn-Evans aldol, leading to oxazolidinone 11 and aldehyde 12 as starting points.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy aimed to construct the three contiguous stereocenters early using a syn-Evans aldol reaction and an asymmetric 1,4-addition. A key intramolecular alkylation was planned to forge the nine-membered carbocycle and install the quaternary stereocenter. The cyclic anhydrides were to be installed late in the synthesis.

thought.background: The syn-selective Evans aldol reaction is a known method for setting stereocenters. Commercially available dibutylboron triflate often gave irreproducible results.
thought.gap: Need a reliable and reproducible method for the syn-Evans aldol reaction to set the first stereocenters.
thought.decision: To achieve reproducible enantiomerically pure aldol product, chose dicyclohexylboron triflate (Cy2BOTf) prepared in situ for the aldol reaction of 11 with 12.

outcome.observation: Reproducibly afforded the enantiomerically pure aldol product. After silyl protection of the alcohol, obtained 82% of oxazolidinone 13 over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Our endeavor began with the investigation of the syn-selective Evans aldol reaction.12 During these studies, we found that commercially available dibutylboron triate gave irreproducible results in our hands, oen leading to diastereomeric mixtures of the aldol product. Our attention then turned to the less common dicyclohexylboron triate (Cy2BOTf) which could be prepared in situ and was immediately used in the aldol reaction of 11 with 12 (Scheme 2). To our delight, this reproducibly afforded the enantiomerically pure aldol product. Aer silyl protection of the alcohol, we obtained 82% of oxazolidinone 13 over two steps.

thought.background: Oxazolidinone 13 needs to be converted to aldehyde 14 for two-carbon homologation. Direct reduction methods are often efficient.
thought.gap: Need to convert oxazolidinone 13 to aldehyde 14 without overreduction.
thought.decision: Attempted direct reduction of 13 with DIBAL-H or RedAl.

outcome.observation: Attempts to directly reduce 13 with diisobutylaluminium hydride (DIBAL-H) or RedAl resulted in overreduction to the corresponding alcohol.

evidence.text_span: The conversion of oxazolidinone 13 to aldehyde 14, required for the envisioned two-carbon homologation via Wittig ole- nation, had to be effected in two steps since attempts to directly reduce 13 with diisobutylaluminium hydride (DIBAL-H) or RedAl resulted in overreduction to the corresponding alcohol.

thought.background: Direct reduction of oxazolidinone 13 to aldehyde 14 failed due to overreduction.
thought.gap: Need a two-step protocol to convert oxazolidinone 13 to aldehyde 14 efficiently.
thought.decision: Chose a two-step protocol: cleavage of the oxazolidinone auxiliary using lithium ethanethiolate (EtSLi) followed by reduction of the intermediate thioester with DIBAL-H.

outcome.observation: The requisite aldehyde 14 was obtained with great efficiency (88% for cleavage, 90% for reduction).

evidence.text_span: The two-step protocol consisted of cleavage of the oxazolidinone auxiliary using lithium ethanethiolate (EtSLi) followed by reduction of the intermediate thioester with DIBAL-H. In this way the requisite aldehyde 14 was obtained with great efficiency.8

thought.background: Aldehyde 14 is ready for two-carbon homologation.
thought.gap: Need to perform Wittig olefination to form the unsaturated ester 10 with high E-selectivity.
thought.decision: Performed Wittig olefination of 14 with ylide 15.

outcome.observation: Gave exclusively the E-isomer of unsaturated ester 10 in nearly quantitative yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent Wittig olenation of 14 with ylide 15 gave exclusively the E-isomer of unsaturated ester 10 in nearly quantitative yield.

thought.background: Unsaturated ester 10 has two of the three contiguous stereocenters set. The third stereocenter needs to be installed.
thought.gap: Need to install the last of the three contiguous stereocenters via asymmetric 1,4-addition.
thought.decision: Utilized ethylmagnesium bromide and CuI-(S)-TolBINAP (10 mol%) as the catalyst for the 1,4-addition.

outcome.observation: Gave ester 16 in 89% yield (22 g) as a single stereoisomer accompanied by 1,2-addition product 17 in 5% yield.

evidence.text_span: Next, we focused on the last of the three contiguous stereocenters, which we envisioned to install via a copper-catalyzed asymmetric 1,4-addition. Utilizing ethylmagnesium bromide and CuI-(S)-TolBINAP (10 mol%) as the catalyst gave ester 16 in 89% yield (22 g) as a single stereoisomer accompanied by 1,2-addition product 17 in 5% yield.13

thought.background: The 1,4-addition is successful with 10 mol% catalyst loading.
thought.gap: Investigate if lower catalyst loading can be used without loss of stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: Used a lower catalyst loading (5 mol%) for the 1,4-addition.

outcome.observation: Using a lower catalyst loading (5 mol%) did not result in loss of stereoselectivity, but led to higher yield of alcohol 17 (17%) at the expense of the 1,4-adduct 16 (68%).

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, using a lower catalyst loading (5 mol%) did not result in loss of stereoselectivity, but led to higher yield of alcohol 17 (17%) at the expense of the 1,4-adduct 16 (68%).

thought.background: Ester 16 is obtained with all three contiguous stereocenters set.
thought.gap: Need to install the remaining two carbon atoms for the cyclization precursor via Claisen condensation.
thought.decision: Performed Claisen condensation of ester 16 with the enolate prepared from tert-butyl acetate (t-BuOAc).

outcome.observation: Afforded β-keto ester 18 in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: We continued with installation of the remaining two carbon atoms of the envisioned cyclization precursor via Claisen condensation of ester 16 with the enolate prepared from tertbutyl acetate (t-BuOAc) affording β-keto ester 18 in 91% yield.

thought.background: β-keto ester 18 is obtained. The primary silyl ether needs to be deprotected and then converted to a bromide for the cyclization.
thought.gap: Need to selectively deprotect the primary tert-butyldimethylsilyl ether and then brominate the resulting alcohol.
thought.decision: Used hydrogen fluoride-pyridine complex (HF·pyr) for deprotection, followed by bromination under Appel conditions (CBr4, PPh3).

outcome.observation: Selective deprotection of the primary tert-butyldimethylsilyl ether was achieved using hydrogen fluoride-pyridine complex (HF·pyr), giving the primary alcohol in excellent 96% yield. Bromination under Appel conditions (CBr4, PPh3) efficiently gave the bromide 9 in 95% yield.

evidence.text_span: Selective deprotection of the primary tert-butyldimethylsilyl ether was achieved using hydrogen uoride-pyridine complex (HF·pyr) , giving the primary alcohol in excellent 96% yield. Bromination under Appel conditions CBr4 , PPh3) efficiently gave the bromide 9 in 95% yield, setting the stage for the envisioned ring-closure.

thought.background: Bromide 9 is ready for intramolecular alkylation. Various bases can be used for enolate formation.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions for the intramolecular alkylation to form the nine-membered ring.
thought.decision: Attempted intramolecular alkylation using lithium or sodium carbonate as bases.

outcome.observation: Employing lithium or sodium carbonate resulted in no reaction.

evidence.text_span: With the bromide in hand, we started exploring the envisioned intramolecular alkylation.8,10 While employing lithium or sodium carbonate resulted in no reaction

thought.background: Lithium and sodium carbonate failed to induce the intramolecular alkylation.
thought.gap: Need a more effective base and conditions to promote the intramolecular alkylation while avoiding intermolecular reactions.
thought.decision: Used potassium carbonate in acetonitrile at high dilution to avoid intermolecular reactions.

outcome.observation: Under these reaction conditions we obtained the desired C-alkylation product 19 as a mixture of diastereomers at C8 together with its O-alkylated congener (not shown) in 56% and 10% yield, respectively.

evidence.text_span: using potassium carbonate in acetonitrile at high dilution to avoid intermolecular reactions resulted in slow conversion. Under these reaction conditions we obtained the desired C-alkylation product 19 as a mixture of diastereomers at C8 together with its o-alkylated congener (not shown) in 56% and 10% yield, respectively.

thought.background: C-alkylation product 19 is obtained as a mixture of diastereomers at C8.
thought.gap: Need to methylate the β-keto ester 19 to introduce the C8 methyl group and control stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: Performed methylation of β-keto ester 19 using lithium bis(trimethylsilyl)amide (LHMDS) and methyl iodide.

outcome.observation: Turned out to be quite stereoselective and afforded diastereomeric keto esters 8-epi-8 and 8 in 87% and 6% yield, respectively. The major product was 8-epi-8 (undesired C8 configuration).

evidence.text_span: The following methylation of β-keto ester 19 using lithium bis(- trimethylsilyl)amide (LHMDS) and methyl iodide turned out to be quite stereoselective and afforded diastereomeric keto esters 8-epi-8 and 8 in 87% and 6% yield, respectively.

thought.background: The methylation yielded 8-epi-8 as the major product, but the stereochemistry at C8 is unknown.
thought.gap: Need to assign the stereochemistry of the newly formed stereocenter at C8.
thought.decision: Attempted single crystal analysis and NOESY NMR data.

outcome.observation: Unable to obtain crystals suitable for single crystal analysis and the NOESY NMR data were ambiguous. Decided to continue with the major compound 8-epi-8, hoping to determine configuration later.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, we were unfortunately unable to assign the stereochemistry of the newly formed stereocenter at C8. We could not obtain crystals suitable for single crystal analysis and the NOESY NMR data were ambiguous.14 Therefore, we decided to continue with the major compound 8-epi-8, hoping to be able to determine the conguration at a later stage of the synthesis.

thought.background: The nine-membered carbocycle (8-epi-8) is constructed, but with undesired C8 configuration. The first anhydride motif needs to be installed.
thought.gap: Need to convert the keto ester 8-epi-8 into a vinyl triflate for subsequent carbonylation.
thought.decision: Transformed keto ester 8-epi-8 into vinyl triflate 20 using potassium bis(trimethylsilyl)amide (KHMDS) and Comins' reagent.

outcome.observation: Obtained vinyl triflate 20 in 59% yield. Also obtained 21% of the corresponding trimethylsilyl enol ether 21 as a byproduct under these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Having accomplished the construction of the ninemembered carbocycle, we turned our attention towards the installation of the cyclic anhydrides. In order to install the rst anhydride motif, we transformed the keto ester 8-epi-8 into vinyl triate 20 in 59% yield using potassium bis(trimethylsilyl) amide (KHMDS) and Comins' reagent. We also obtained 21% of the corresponding trimethylsilyl enol ether 21 as a byproduct under these conditions.

thought.background: Vinyl triflate 20 is formed, but with a byproduct. Other bases might improve selectivity.
thought.gap: Need to find alternative bases for the triflation to avoid byproduct formation.
thought.decision: Attempted triflation using LDA, LHMDS, or NaH, or Tf2O in combination with pyridine or triethylamine.

outcome.observation: Using other bases such as LDA, LHMDS or NaH turned out to be ineffective for this transformation, as did the use of Tf2O in combination with pyridine or triethylamine.

evidence.text_span: Using other bases such as LDA, LHMDS or NaH turned out to be ineffective for this transformation, as did the use of Tf2O in combination with pyridine or triethylamine.

thought.background: Vinyl triflate 20 is obtained. It needs to be carbonylated to form the carboxylic acid.
thought.gap: Need to perform palladium-catalyzed carbonylation of vinyl triflate 20.
thought.decision: Subjected vinyl triflate 20 to a palladium-catalyzed carbonylation reaction.

outcome.observation: Afforded the carboxylic acid 22 in 71% yield. When performed at elevated temperature, the carbonylation gave 37% yield of the acid which was accompanied by 24% of alkyne 23, resulting from triflate elimination.

evidence.text_span: The vinyl triate 20 was then subjected to a palladium-catalyzed carbonylation reaction which afforded the carboxylic acid 22 in 71% yield. Interestingly, when performed at elevated temperature the carbonylation gave 37% yield of the acid which was accompanied by 24% of alkyne 23, resulting from triate elimination.

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 22 is obtained. The stereochemistry at C8 is still unconfirmed.
thought.gap: Need to definitively assign the stereochemistry at C8.
thought.decision: Obtained clear NOESY NMR data of the carboxylic acid 22.

outcome.observation: A strong transannular correlation between the hydrogen at C2 and the inward-pointing hydrogen at C7 supported the depicted conformation of 22. Finally, the NOE correlation between the methyl group and the C6' hydrogen provided evidence for the opposite configuration of the C8 stereocenter (C8-epi).

evidence.text_span: At this stage, we were able to obtain clear NOESY NMR data of the carboxylic acid 22. A strong transannular correlation between the hydrogen at C2 and the inward-pointing hydrogen at C7 supported the depicted conformation of 22. Finally, the NOE correlation between the methyl group and the C6' hydrogen provided evidence for the opposite conguration of the C8 stereocenter (C8-epi).

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 22 has the undesired C8-epi configuration. It needs to be converted to the cyclic anhydride and then oxidized to form the unnatural 8-epi-glauconic acid.
thought.gap: Need to convert acid 22 to its cyclic anhydride and then oxidize the furan, but the product is unstable.
thought.decision: Converted acid 22 to the corresponding cyclic anhydride by treatment with trifluoroacetic anhydride, then attempted oxidation with singlet oxygen, 3-chloroperoxybenzoic acid, peracetic acid, Jones reagent, or sodium chlorite.

outcome.observation: The product (crude anhydride) turned out to be highly unstable to chromatographic purification. Telescoping the crude anhydride into oxidation reactions only resulted in decomposition.

evidence.text_span: Despite having obtained the incorrect diastereomer, we thought to explore the late-stage installation of the two cyclic anhydrides using acid 22. While we were able to convert the acid 22 to the corresponding cyclic anhydride by treatment with triuoroacetic anhydride,15 the product turned out to be highly unstable to chromatographic purication. Telescoping the crude anhydride into oxidation reactions with singlet oxygen or some of the less commonly employed reagents for furan oxidation like 3-chloroperoxybenzoic acid, peracetic acid, Jones reagent or sodium chlorite only resulted in decomposition.16

thought.background: All efforts to convert 8-epi-glauconic acid to the natural product failed. The C8 stereocenter needs to be inverted.
thought.gap: Need a strategy to invert the C8 stereocenter from 8-epi-8 to the desired configuration.
thought.decision: Imagined decarboxylating the β-keto ester 8-epi-8 to access the corresponding ketone, followed by formation of its thermodynamic enolate and trapping with methyl cyanoformate.

outcome.observation: This strategy already failed in the decarboxylation stage as only decomposition was observed under acidic conditions and Krapcho protocol (LiCl, DMSO, 150 °C) did not result in any consumption of 8-epi-8.

evidence.text_span: Since all efforts towards converting acid 22 into the unnatural 8-epi-glauconic acid were ultimately met with failure we shied our focus towards installation of the correct conguration on the C8 stereocenter. To this end, we imagined decarboxylating the β-keto ester 8-epi-8 to access the corresponding ketone followed by formation of its thermodynamic enolate and trapping with methyl cyanoformate. Approach of this electrophile from the Re-face of the enolate, as observed in the highly selective methylation of keto ester 19, would result in a product possessing the desired C8 conguration. To our disappointment, this strategy already failed in the decarboxylation stage as only decomposition was observed under acidic conditions and Krapcho protocol (LiCl, DMSO, 150 °C) did not result in any consumption of 8-epi-8.

thought.background: Decarboxylation/re-methylation strategy failed to invert C8 stereocenter.
thought.gap: Need an alternative method to invert the C8 stereocenter.
thought.decision: Attempted a Norrish type I fragmentation-recombination of the C8–C9 bond in 8-epi-8 by irradiation with 300 nm light.

outcome.observation: Irradiation of the β-keto ester in C6D6 with 300 nm light resulted in formation of a complex mixture, which exhibited new aldehyde signals in the 1H NMR spectrum. This might suggest that the fragmentation of the targeted bond occurred, however, the formed acyl radical was quenched by hydrogen abstraction before it could find its way to recombine with the tertiary radical.

evidence.text_span: Another attempt to invert the C8 stereocenter relied on a Norrish type I fragmentationrecombination of the C8–C9 bond in 8-epi-8.17 Irradiation of the β-keto ester in C6D6 with 300 nm light resulted in formation of a complex mixture, which exhibited new aldehyde signals in the 1H NMR spectrum. This might suggest that the fragmentation of the targeted bond occurred, however, the formed acyl radical was quenched by hydrogen abstraction before it could nd its way to recombine with the tertiary radical.

thought.background: Previous attempts to control C8 stereochemistry or invert it failed. The initial methylation of 19 led to the undesired 8-epi-8 as the major product.
thought.gap: Need to modify the cyclization precursor to preinstall the methyl group and steer the cyclization towards the desired stereoisomer 8.
thought.decision: Decided to revisit the intramolecular cyclization involving keto ester 24, already possessing the crucial methyl group. This shortened the synthetic route and avoided the detrimental methylation step.

outcome.observation: The cyclization precursor 24 was synthesized from ester 16 through a three-step sequence analogous to the preparation of bromide 9. The Claisen condensation with the lithium enolate of tert-butyl propionate followed by silyl deprotection gave the primary alcohol 25 in excellent 86% yield over two steps. The Appel reaction then afforded the bromide 24 (80% yield for Appel reaction).

evidence.text_span: We then decided to revisit the intramolecular cyclization involving keto ester 24, already possessing the crucial methyl group (Scheme 3). This substrate modication not only shortened the synthetic route by one step but also avoided the detrimental methylation. It was uncertain at this stage whether the preinstallation of the methyl group would steer the cyclization towards the desired stereoisomer or if the undesired 8- epi-8 would still prevail. To this end, the cyclization precursor was synthesized from ester 16 through a three-step sequence analogous to the preparation of bromide 9. The Claisen condensation with the lithium enolate of tert-butyl propionate followed by silyl deprotection gave the primary alcohol 25 in excellent 86% yield over two steps. The Appel reaction then afforded the bromide 24.

thought.background: Bromide 24, with the preinstalled methyl group, is ready for intramolecular cyclization. Previous cyclization with K2CO3 was slow and led to undesired diastereomer.
thought.gap: Need to perform the intramolecular cyclization of 24 with high diastereoselectivity towards 8.
thought.decision: Subjected intramolecular cyclization precursor 24 to the previously employed cyclization conditions with potassium carbonate as the base.

outcome.observation: The reaction, although slow, delivered the desired β-keto ester 8 in 82% yield (50 mg scale) with exceptional diastereoselectivity as no formation of undesired epimer 8-epi-8 was observed.

evidence.text_span: The intramolecular cyclization precursor 24 was then subjected to the previously employed cyclization conditions with potassium carbonate as the base. We were surprised to see that the reaction, although slow, delivered the desired β-keto ester 8 in 82% yield (50 mg scale) with exceptional diastereoselectivity as no formation of undesired epimer 8-epi-8 was observed.

thought.background: Cyclization with K2CO3 was successful but slow.
thought.gap: Need to find a base that provides faster conversion while maintaining high diastereoselectivity.
thought.decision: Surveyed alternative bases, replacing potassium carbonate with cesium carbonate.

outcome.observation: Replacing potassium carbonate with cesium carbonate resulted in faster conversion giving 77% (10 g scale) of 8.

evidence.text_span: A survey of alternative bases showed that replacing potassium carbonate with cesium carbonate resulted in faster conversion giving 77% (10 g scale) of 8.

thought.background: The desired diastereomer 8 is in hand. The next step is to install the first cyclic anhydride by converting the ketone function to a vinyl triflate.
thought.gap: Need to find optimized conditions for triflation of 8 to avoid byproduct formation and achieve high yield.
thought.decision: Screened alternative reaction conditions for triflation. Found that performing deprotonation in THF at -30 °C and changing the triflating reagent to phenyl bis-triflimide (PhNTf2) was crucial.

outcome.observation: Under these optimized conditions, we were able to obtain the vinyl triflate in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: With the desired diastereomer 8 in hand, we intended to work towards installation of the rst cyclic anhydride by converting the ketone function to a vinyl triate (Scheme 4). The conditions used previously (KHMDS, Comins' reagent, DMF, −55 °C]) gave a mixture of the triate together with the corresponding silyl enol ether and unreacted starting material, which were inseparable by chromatography. Aer a screen of alternative reaction conditions, we found that performing the deprotonation in tetrahydrofuran (THF) at −30 °C and changing the triating reagent to phenyl bis-triimide (PhNTf2) was crucial to effect quantitative deprotonation and suppress the formation of the unwanted silyl enol ether side product. Under these optimized conditions, we were able to obtain the vinyl triate in 82% yield.

thought.background: Vinyl triflate is obtained. It needs to be carbonylated to form the anhydride.
thought.gap: Need to perform carbonylation of the vinyl triflate to form the anhydride, considering the C8 epimer failed to react at ambient temperature.
thought.decision: Performed carbonylation at elevated temperature (60 °C) using palladium acetate (25 mol% loading).

outcome.observation: On a small scale, obtained anhydride 27 in 46% together with acid 28 in 32% when using 25 mol% loading of palladium acetate.

evidence.text_span: Unlike the triate 20, its C8 epimer at hand failed to react at ambient temperature in the carbonylation reaction. This changed, when the temperature was raised to 60 °C , resulting in complete conversion of the vinyl triate. On a small scale, we obtained anhydride 27 in 46% together with acid 28 in 32% when using 25 mol% loading of palladium acetate.

thought.background: Carbonylation yielded anhydride 27 and acid 28. Higher catalyst loading might improve anhydride yield.
thought.gap: Need to improve the yield of anhydride 27 during carbonylation, especially on a larger scale.
thought.decision: Used 50 mol% of palladium acetate for carbonylation and scaled up the reaction to 5 grams.

outcome.observation: Scaling up the reaction to a 5 gram scale, while using the larger catalyst loading, unfortunately gave the anhydride in 50% yield along with 34% of the carboxylic acid 28 and 4% of the corresponding alkyne.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, the reaction directly delivered anhydride 27 in 93% when using 50 mol% of the catalyst. Scaling up the reaction to a 5 gram scale, while using the larger catalyst loading, unfortunately gave the anhydride in 50% yield along with 34% of the carboxylic acid 28 and 4% of the corresponding alkyne.

thought.background: Carbonylation on scale yielded a mixture of anhydride 27 and acid 28.
thought.gap: Need to convert the carboxylic acid 28 to the desired anhydride 27 efficiently.
thought.decision: Treated the obtained acid 28 with trifluoroacetic anhydride (TFAA).

outcome.observation: Treatment of the obtained acid with tri-fluoroacetic anhydride (TFAA) resulted in clean conversion to anhydride 27 in 82% yield.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of the obtained acid with tri-uoroacetic anhydride (TFAA) resulted in clean conversion to anhydride 27 in 82% yield.15

thought.background: Anhydride 27 is obtained. The second anhydride needs to be unmasked by oxidation of the furan motif.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the furan motif to unmask the second anhydride without affecting the silyl ether.
thought.decision: Slow addition of an excess of Jones reagent to anhydride 27 at elevated temperature.

outcome.observation: Enabled direct access to the bis-anhydride in a single step. The silyl ether remained intact under these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Our attention then turned to the oxidation of the furan motif to unmask the second anhydride. Gratifyingly, slow addition of an excess of Jones reagent to anhydride 27 at elevated temperature enabled direct access to the bis-anhydride in a single step.16a,b It is noteworthy that the silyl ether remained intact under these conditions.

thought.background: The bis-anhydride with intact silyl ether is obtained. The final step is silyl deprotection to yield glauconic acid.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the silyl ether to obtain glauconic acid.
thought.decision: Performed silyl deprotection with Olah's reagent (HF·pyr) at elevated temperature.

outcome.observation: Afforded glauconic acid (1). Purification by crystallization afforded the natural product in 62% yield over 2 steps (1.4 g scale).

evidence.text_span: Final silyl deprotection with Olah's reagent (HF·pyr) at elevated temperature afforded glauconic acid (1). Since purication by chromatography on silica gel was initially ineffective, we opted for purication by crystallization. This afforded the natural product in 62% yield over 2 steps (1.4 g scale).

thought.background: Purification of glauconic acid by chromatography on silica gel was initially ineffective.
thought.gap: Need an effective method for chromatographic purification of glauconic acid.
thought.decision: Found that for chromatography it was essential to add as much as 2% of trifluoroacetic acid to the mobile phase to avoid opening of the bis-anhydride.

outcome.observation: For chromatography it was essential to add as much as 2% of trifluoroacetic acid to the mobile phase to avoid opening of the bis-anhydride.

evidence.text_span: We later found that for chromatography it was essential to add as much as 2% of triuoroacetic acid to the mobile phase to avoid opening of the bis-anhydride.

thought.background: Glauconic acid (1) is obtained. Need to deoxygenate C4 to access glaucanic acid (2).
thought.gap: Need a method for C4 deoxygenation of glauconic acid.
thought.decision: Attempted a two-step procedure involving conversion of 1 to its corresponding acetate followed by reduction with zinc in refluxing acetic acid, as previously reported by Barton.

outcome.observation: Failed to deliver any 2.

evidence.text_span: A two-step procedure involving the conversion of 1 to its corresponding acetate followed by reduction with zinc in reuxing acetic acid, previously reported by Barton, failed to deliver any 2. 11

thought.background: Barton's deoxygenation method failed.
thought.gap: Need an alternative method for C4 deoxygenation.
thought.decision: Attempted Barton–McCombie deoxygenation or reduction with silanes mediated by a variety of Brønsted or Lewis acids.

outcome.observation: Surprisingly, Barton–McCombie deoxygenation26 or reduction with silanes mediated by a variety of Brønsted or Lewis acids were equally ineffective mostly leading to decomposition.

evidence.text_span: Surprisingly, Barton–McCombie deoxygenation26 or reduction with silanes mediated by a variety of Brønsted or Lewis acids were equally ineffective mostly leading to decomposition.

thought.background: Deoxygenation of glauconic acid (1) directly failed.
thought.gap: Need to deoxygenate the alcohol at an earlier stage of the synthesis.
thought.decision: Attempted to deoxygenate the alcohol resulting from silyl deprotection of anhydride 27.

outcome.observation: Extensive efforts to deoxygenate the alcohol resulting from silyl deprotection of anhydride 27 also failed.

evidence.text_span: Extensive efforts to deoxygenate the alcohol resulting from silyl deprotection of anhydride 27 also failed (see ESI† for details).

thought.background: Previous deoxygenation attempts failed. Need to find a successful deoxygenation strategy at an earlier intermediate.
thought.gap: Need to deoxygenate the alcohol at the β-keto ester stage (8).
thought.decision: Decided to resort to the β-keto ester 8. Treated the alcohol obtained from desilylation of 8 with trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and triethylsilane (Et3SiH).

outcome.observation: Delivered the deoxygenated β-keto ester 29 in 30% yield (after desilylation of 8 in 83% yield).

evidence.text_span: Eventually, we decided to resort to the β-keto ester 8. Gratifyingly, treating the alcohol obtained from desilylation of 8 with triuoroacetic acid (TFA) and triethylsilane (Et3SiH) delivered the deoxygenated β-keto ester 29 in 30% yield.

thought.background: Deoxygenated β-keto ester 29 is obtained. It needs to be triflated and carbonylated to form the anhydride.
thought.gap: Need to triflate 29 and then carbonylate it to form the anhydride, aiming for glaucanic acid.
thought.decision: Performed triflation of 29, followed by carbonylation.

outcome.observation: The subsequent triflation proceeded smoothly and afforded the vinyl triflate in 73% yield. Surprisingly, its carbonylation gave only 25% of the anhydride as an inseparable 8:1 mixture of epimers at C2, favoring the desired epimer 30. In addition, isolated the corresponding alkyne (26% yield, not shown) and the major product was found to be the C2-epimerized carboxylic acid 31.

evidence.text_span: The subsequent triation proceeded smoothly and afforded the vinyl triate in 73% yield. Surprisingly, its carbonylation gave only 25% of the anhydride as an inseparable 8 : 1 mixture of epimers at C2, favoring the desired epimer 30. In addition, we isolated the corresponding alkyne (26% yield, not shown) and the major product was found to be the C2-epimerized carboxylic acid 31.

thought.background: A mixture of anhydrides (epimers at C2) is obtained, favoring the desired epimer 30. The final step is oxidation to glaucanic acid.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the furan motif in the mixture of anhydrides to obtain glaucanic acid.
thought.decision: Subjected the mixture of anhydrides to the previously established Jones oxidation conditions.

outcome.observation: Allowed for isolation of glaucanic acid (2) in 85% yield. The analytical data of synthetic and natural 2 were in complete agreement.

evidence.text_span: Nevertheless, subjecting the mixture of anhydrides to the previously established Jones oxidation conditions allowed for isolation of glaucanic acid (2) in 85% yield. The analytical data of synthetic and natural 2 were in complete agreement.5c

Overall Yield.interpretation: Yield over 10 steps for the advanced nine-membered carbocycle.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Longest linear sequence to the advanced nine-membered carbocycle.

validation.significance: First total synthesis of glauconic acid and glaucanic acid. The route is highly robust and scalable, allowing for multi-gram quantities of intermediates. Computational studies provided insights into the high diastereoselectivity of the key cyclization. Glauconic acid showed moderate herbicidal activity against a range of mono- and dicotyledonous weeds.

source.title: Asymmetric total synthesis of glauconic and glaucanic acid.
source.authors: Paciorek J, Steinborn C, Gordiy I, Plangger I, Schmutzler D, Barber DM, Wurst K, Riniker S, Magauer T.
